section.name: dotfiles-privacy
section.summary: What must never be committed to the dotfiles repository: SSH private keys, shell history, .env files, GPG keys, and Claude Code project configs.

rule.statement: Never commit SSH private keys (id_rsa, id_ed25519, id_ecdsa) even if they have a passphrase; commit only .ssh/config.
rule.rationale: A passphrase-protected private key can be brute-forced offline once exfiltrated from a public or semi-public git repository. The SSH config file (host aliases, identity file paths) contains no secrets.

rule.statement: Never commit .bash_history or .zsh_history; they may contain passwords typed directly on the command line, API tokens from curl commands, or secret paths.
rule.rationale: Shell history is a rich source of inadvertent secret leakage: API keys passed as -H flags, passwords typed instead of using a variable, SSH commands with usernames. History files belong in .gitignore.

rule.statement: Never commit Claude Code project configs (.claude/projects/); they may contain project-specific instructions referencing internal paths, API endpoints, or credential patterns.
rule.rationale: Claude project configs are machine- and project-specific. They accumulate references to local file paths, internal service URLs, and sometimes credentials captured during setup. They should live locally, not in a shared repo.

list.item: SAFE: .ssh/config (host aliases, identity file paths, ServerAlive settings)
SAFE: .gitconfig (user name, email, aliases, editor preference)

section.reference: .gitignore for the dotfiles repo covering all sensitive paths

antipattern.description: Using a public GitHub repository for dotfiles without a comprehensive .gitignore.
antipattern.reason: Public dotfiles repos are indexed by search engines and scraped by secret-scanning tools. A single committed private key or API token is a permanent breach even if deleted later (git history).
